retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: World Health Organisation created a priority list of pathogens in 2017 and 2020 based on their substantial threat to morbidity and mortality. The priority list is categorized into three tiers: [15] [16] Tuberculosis: A Global Priority for research and development especially Multidrug-Resistant Tuberculosis Critical Priority: Carbapenem-Resistant Acinetobacter baumannii Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas Aeruginosa Carbapenem-Resistant, Third generation Cephalosporins-Resistant Enterobacteriaceae High Priority Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus faecium Vancomycin-Resistant, Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus Clarithromycin-Resistant H.Pylori Fluoroquinolones-Resistant Campylobacter species Fluoroquinolones-Resistant Salmonella species Third generation Cephalosporins-Resistant, Fluoroquinolones-Resistant Neisseria gonorrhoeae Medium Priority Penicillin-non-susceptible Streptococcus pneumoniae Ampicillin-Resistant Hemophilus influenzae Fluoroquinolones-Resistant Shigella species

retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Between 1935 and 2003, fourteen new classes of antibiotics were introduced. Since 1998, only ten new antibiotics were approved, of which only linezolid and daptomycin have new targets of action. [1] According to the World Health Organization(WHO), antibiotic pipeline data report 2021, and eleven new antibiotics have been approved since 2017. Only two of them represent a new class and have a new target of action. They include vaborbactam+meropenem and lefamulin. The likely reason behind this is that the development of antibiotics is risky, expensive, and less profitable than the drugs to treat chronic diseases. With the slow development of antimicrobials, accelerated emergence, and spread of resistant organisms, antimicrobial stewardship is of utmost importance to optimize the use of existing antimicrobials. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Antibiotic Resistance -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Recognition of issues related to the use of antibiotics has been present since their early clinical introduction in the 1940s. Since then, the use of antimicrobials and often inappropriate use of these have been increasing. Antibiotic resistance in the United States kills approximately 23,000 patients a year and incurs over $20 billion in additional medical expenses. Antibiotic stewardship was established to combat this trend and was recognized in 1996 to draw attention to the rising incidents in mortality and morbidity associated with inappropriate use of antibiotics. Antimicrobial agents are at least partially responsible for the development of serious infections, such as Staphylococcus aureus, vancomycin-resistant enterococci, extended-spectrum B-lactamase producing Enterobacteriaceae, and other infectious agents. The focus of the stewardship programs is to improve clinical outcomes, decrease antibiotic resistance, and decrease healthcare costs. In 2007, stewardship programs were nationally recognized and reinforced by the publication of the stewardship guidelines from the Infectious Disease Society of America (IDSA) in association with the Society of Healthcare Epidemiology of America (SHEA). These guidelines were helpful in developing an institutional program to enhance antimicrobial stewardship. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococci -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to the National Healthcare Safety Network, enterococci species ranked as the second overall healthcare-associated infection between 2011 and 2014. In particular, E. Faecalis was a cause in 7.4% of cases. Furthermore, Enterococci species were found to be the number one isolate for central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSI), number three for catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI), number eleven for ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and number two for surgical site infections (SSI). While the majority of Enterococcus isolates are E. faecalis , the majority of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus isolates are E. faecium . From 2011 to 2014 the resistance of E. faecium to vancomycin was as high as 83.8 % of isolates for CLABSI and 86.2 % for CAUTI. In 2013 the CDC categorized vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus as a “serious threat,” suggesting the need for increased monitoring and prevention activities.

retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The CDC's 2019 Antibiotic resistance Threat report has shown an 18% overall decline in deaths from AMR compared to the 2013 report and a decline in deaths by AMR by 28% in-hospital patients. Similarly, the report has shown a 41% decline in VRE, 33% in Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter, 29% in multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa, 25% in Candida, and 21% in MRSA. Although the results for high-priority pathogens are promising, the report also highlighted a 315% increase in erythromycin-resistant invasive group A Streptococcus, 124% in drug-resistant gonorrhea, and 50% in ESBL Enterobacteriaceae. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the antibiotic susceptibilities, Methicillin resistance in S. aureus is defined as an oxacillin minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of greater than or equal to 4 micrograms/mL. MRSA infection is one of the leading causes of hospital-acquired infections and is commonly associated with significant morbidity, mortality, length of stay, and cost burden. MRSA infections can be further divided into hospital-associated (HA-MRSA) infections and community-associated (CA-MRSA) infections. They differ not only in respect to their clinical features and molecular biology but also to their antibiotic susceptibility and treatment. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of MRSA and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the recognition and management of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Moment 2 is to think about what cultures should be obtained and the best empiric treatment. This moment focuses on the need for culture data when appropriate before administering the antibiotics. When no specific data is available, there is a tendency for prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy. The second part of Moment 2 ensures timely administration of empirical therapy based on severity and likely source of infection. For example, patients are at low risk of MRSA for urinary tract infections. Therefore they would not benefit from IV vancomycin. Local antibiotics guidelines should be developed for common inpatient infections- this aids in enacting Moment 2.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 3: Use of Engineering and Work Practice Controls to Reduce the Opportunity for Patient and Healthcare Worker Exposure to Potentially Infectious Material
retrieved_docs.content: Antimicrobial stewardship programs help control antimicrobial resistance, improve outcomes, and reduce healthcare costs. Antimicrobial stewardship should be programmed to monitor antimicrobial susceptibility profiles, anticipating and assessing any new antimicrobial resistance patterns. These trends should be correlated with the antimicrobial agents used to assess susceptibility patterns. [28] Antimicrobial stewardship programs can be designed as either active or passive initiatives, targeting either pre-prescription or post-prescription periods. An active program includes prescription restrictions and preauthorization in the pre-prescription period, whereas passive initiatives include education, guidelines, and antimicrobial susceptibility reports. An active post-prescription program focuses on providing real-time feedback to clinicians regarding antibiotic usage, dose, bioavailability, and susceptibility, with automatic conversion of intravenous to oral formulations. In contrast, a passive post-prescription program involves integrating electronic medical records to generate alerts for prolonged prescriptions and mismatches between antibiotics and microorganisms. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Vancomycin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of vancomycin. Determine the FDA-approved indications and off-label uses for vancomycin therapy. Assess the potential adverse effects associated with vancomycin administration. Determine the importance of collaboration and coordination among interprofessional team members for enhancing patient care when administering and monitoring vancomycin therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hospital-Acquired Infections(Archived) -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics change during an acute illness, especially during sepsis. For a given drug, the volume of distribution varies depending on the phase of the illness or recovery. This is an important concept and must be considered when determining dosing medication dosings, especially for drugs like aminoglycosides and beta-lactams. Another important determinant of the dosing of renal-excreted medications is the estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), which may be impaired in sepsis. The IDSA and ATS recommend selecting antibiotic dosing based on pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics for critically ill patients with ventilator-associated Pneumonia. [21] An antibiotic's efficacy may be concentration-dependent (fluoroquinolones and aminoglycosides), time-dependent (beta-lactams), or a combination of both (Vancomycin). Time-dependent drugs are administered as extended infusions to maintain concentrations within 4 times the minimum inhibitory concentrations. In contrast, concentration-dependent drugs are dosed targeting 1 high peak (with a 1-time high dose) or multiple peaks with intermittent dosing.

retrieved_docs.title: Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococci -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus. Review the presentation of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus. Summarize the treatment considerations for vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus. Explain interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and outcomes in patients with vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The spectrum of infectious diseases is rapidly evolving. Emerging infectious agents present with a constellation of challenges. The highly virulent pathogens with increased resistance result in increased morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. It has been estimated that ten million people will die every year due to AMR by 2050. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tobramycin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: To ensure appropriate prescribing practices, involving a board-certified infectious disease pharmacist with access to the latest antibiogram data is beneficial. Their expertise can help guide the selection and optimal use of tobramycin based on local resistance patterns. This is a vital aspect of antibiotic stewardship, emphasizing responsible antibiotic use.

retrieved_docs.content: Vancomycin (vankomycin), a glycoside belonging to the glycopeptide antibacterial family, shares the indication for pseudomembranous colitis. Ethacrynic acid, an aryloxyacetic acid derivative classified as a high‑ceiling diuretic, is also a genitourinary (loop) diuretic; tikrynafen is another member of the aryloxyacetic acid derivative class. Fosfomycin is a urinary anti‑infective agent used for urinary tract infections and is listed among the “other antibacterials in ATC.” Telavancin is a member of the glycopeptide antibacterials group. Imipenem, a carbapenem antibiotic, is routinely employed in Kirby‑Bauer susceptibility testing and minimum bactericidal concentration determinations, and it is effective against serious infections such as bone infections, bacterial meningitis, and cocci infections, reflecting its broad Gram‑positive and Gram‑negative activity. Glycopeptide antibacterials (e.g., vancomycin/vankomycin) and systemic nitrofuran derivatives are also ISA members of “other antibacterials in ATC.” Vancomycin is a descendant of the glycoside hierarchy, is linked to telavancin, and is employed for pseudomembranous colitis caused by Clostridioides difficile. Fidaxomicin is classified as an intestinal antibiotic and is likewise indicated for pseudomembranous colitis. The intestinal anti‑infective branch comprises imidazole derivatives, general intestinal antibiotics and sulfonamide intestinal anti‑infectives, all ISA members of the higher‑level category “intestinal antiinfectives.” Nystatin and vancomycin are grouped with other intestinal antibiotics. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1653
retrieved_docs.content: Cefaklor serves as an antibacterial therapeutic agent, being employed to treat bacterial infections through its antimicrobial activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics for systemic use encompass a group of tuberculostatic antibiotics, referred to in Czech as “antibiotika tuberkulostatická.” This subclass of systemic antibiotics is specifically employed to inhibit the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis and is often used in multidrug‑resistant or refractory tuberculosis regimens. Within the tuberculostatic category, both cykloserin (cycloserine) and viomycin are recognized agents; each exemplifies a distinct chemical type that shares the common therapeutic purpose of tuberculostatic activity. By incorporating cykloserin and viomycin, the broader class of antibiotics for systemic use provides clinicians with alternative options for targeting persistent mycobacterial infections, thereby enhancing the flexibility and effectiveness of tuberculosis treatment strategies.

This classification links the two agents to the broader group of aminoglycoside antibiotics, indicating that they share pharmacologic characteristics typical of this antimicrobial category.

Its activity against these Gram‑negative bacteria makes it an appropriate therapeutic option for severe or resistant infections involving either organism.

The glycopeptide subclass comprises vancomycin (vankomycin) and telavancin; both are used for Clostridioides difficile‑related pseudomembranous colitis, with vancomycin additionally classified among intestinal antibiotics and serving as a child of the glycoside lineage. Fidaxomicin, another intestinal antibiotic, provides targeted oral therapy for the same infection. Other systemic agents listed under “other antibacterials in ATC” include bacitracin, clofoctol, daptomycin, tedizolid and telavancin. Daptomycin is correctly classified as a lipopeptide antibiotic, not an aminoglycoside. Intestinal antiinfectives form a separate subclass with isa relationships to imidazole derivatives, sulfonamides and broader intestinal antibiotics, reflecting their primary use for gastrointestinal infections. The “antibiotics, intestinal” pharmacologic category includes agents such as rifaximin and streptomycin,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics refer to a group of antimicrobial agents used to treat infections within the gastrointestinal tract, and this group includes both nystatin and fidaxomicin. Nystatin is an antifungal medication that targets fungal organisms in the gut, while fidaxomicin is a narrow‑spectrum antibacterial drug primarily employed against Clostridioides difficile infection. By classifying nystatin and fidaxomicin as members of the intestinal antibiotic category, clinicians recognize their specific roles in managing gastrointestinal microbial disorders and can select the appropriate agent based on the type of pathogen involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Clofoctol, daptomycin, and tedizolid are all classified as members of the ATC category “other antibacterials.” This grouping indicates that each of these agents is recognized as an antibacterial drug that does not fit into the more specific ATC sub‑classes for antibiotics, and they are therefore grouped together for purposes of pharmacological classification, regulatory reporting, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 61
retrieved_docs.content: topical” while also being encompassed by the broader “antibiotika” class, illustrating its dual classification as a specific topical antifungal antibiotic and as a general antibiotic product. Within the anti‑infective hierarchy, the subgroup “aminoglykosidy” is a child of “anti‑infective agents for bacteria, viruses and fungi” and is linked to “aminoglycoside antibacterials” via an inverse ISA relationship. Aminoglykosidy lists spectinomycin as its parent, has a child class “glykosidy,” and is mapped to the newer agent telavancin, thereby connecting older glycoside‑based antibiotics with newer therapeutics. Spectinomycin itself is an aminoglycoside antibacterial and a heterocyclic tricyclic compound; bacterial susceptibility to spectinomycin is commonly evaluated with the Kirby‑Bauer test. The general class “antibiotika” encompasses a wide hierarchy of agents. Beta‑lactam antibiotics (amoxicillin, ampicillin, cefalexin, cefazolin) are child members that share a cell‑wall synthesis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1430
retrieved_docs.content: Cefaklor and paromomycin each fulfill an antibacterial therapeutic role, serving as antibiotics that are employed to treat bacterial infections. Their shared function highlights their importance in antimicrobial therapy and underscores their relevance in managing bacterial diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics encompass a range of specific antimicrobial agents, including cefalexin, cefotetan, and ertapenem, each representing a distinct member of the broader antibiotic category. Cefalexin is a first‑generation cephalosporin, cefotetan is a second‑generation cephalosporin, and ertapenem belongs to the carbapenem class; all are employed to treat bacterial infections. Recognizing that these drugs are individual children of the antibiotic family aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy based on the infection’s spectrum, resistance patterns, and pharmacologic properties.

Urinary anti‑infectives include fosfomycin, highlighting its role in treating urinary‑tract infections. Imipenem, a carbapenem antibacterial, is evaluated by Kirby‑Bauer and minimum bactericidal concentration assays and is indicated for serious invasive infections such as bone infections, bacterial meningitis and coccal infections. Within the aminoglycoside family, streptomycin is a child compound and micronomicin is an ISA type; both are linked to the broader glykosidy chemical class, which also maps to the drug odiparcil. Streptomycin is occasionally noted in intestinal antibiotic formulations, illustrating overlap between systemic and gastrointestinal applications. retrieved_docs.content: Ampicilin and cefazolin are both classified as members of the antibiotic group, serving as child entities within the broader category of antibiotics. Recognizing that ampicilin and cefazolin belong to this same therapeutic class helps clinicians understand their related antimicrobial properties and informs appropriate selection of antibacterial treatment.
